coredata.dc:title: Magnetically controlled fluorescence biosensor for simultaneous detection of aflatoxin B1 and its toxin-producing gene aflD

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yaqi
Peng, Shuangfeng
Chen, Xin
Sun, Danni
Zuo, Xiang
Liu, Chang
Zhang, Qi
Li, Shijie
Ye, Hua
Kong, Dezhao
coredata.dc:description: The aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was considered as the most toxic natural compound and exists widely in the world, it is great significance to develop a high-performance sensor for simultaneously detection AFB1 and its toxin-producing gene aflD. In this paper, a magnetically controlled fluorescent sensor was developed, ssDNA1/ssDNA2 was modified on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/Mn:ZnS quantum dots (QDs) as an energy donor, and cDNA/Apt modified by black hole quencher (BHQ) was coupled with MNPs as an energy acceptor, which leaded to fluorescence quenching through donor-acceptor energy transfer. Parts of fluorescent probes were separated from magnetic carriers when the sensor was incubated with the targets. After magnetic separation, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe released from the supernatant was positively correlated with the target concentration. The simultaneous detection of aflD and AFB1 was realized. The sensor displayed a wide detection range (0.1–500 nM and 0.1–500 ng/mL) for aflD and AFB1, with detection limits of 0.021 nM and 0.038 ng/mL for aflD and AFB1. The magnetic fluorescence biosensor showed a good recovery rate in real rice flour and corn flour samples. The magnetic control fluorescence biosensor had the high sensitivity and specificity. This strategy provided a simple, efficient and sensitive method for food safety monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Double quantum dots
Magnetic nano-materials Fe3O4
AFB1
aflD
DNA hybridization synchronous detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Biosensor fabrication and the detection process
Principle of simultaneous detection of AFB1 and aflD
Characterization of MNPs and coupling characterization
Simultaneous detection of aflD and AFB1

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of QDs1. (B) The TEM image of QDs1 (Illustration: Particle size distribution of QDs1). (C) The FTIR image of QDs1. (D) X-ray photoelectron spectrum of QDs1. (E) Particle size distribution of QDs1. (F) C1s spectrum of QDs1,(C) O1s spectrum of QDs1.

(A) Fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of QDs2. (B) The TEM image of QDs2 (Illustration: Particle size distribution of QDs2). (C) The FTIR image of QDs. (D) X-ray photoelectron spectrum of QDs2. (E) S2p spectrum of QDs2. (F) C1s spectrum of QDs2. (G) O1s spectrum of QDs2. (H) Mn2p spectrum of QDs2. (I) Zn2p spectrum of QDs2.

(A) The TEM image of MNPs (Illustration: Particle size distribution of MNPs). (B) The FTIR image of MNPs. (C) X-ray photoelectron spectrum of MNPs. (D) C1s spectrum of MNPs, (E) O1s spectrum of MNPs. (F) Fe2p spectrum of MNPs.

(A) optimizes the concentration ratio of QDs1 and QDs2. (B) Effects of ssDNA1 and ssDNA2 on fluorescence intensity. (C) The influence of different buffer solutions on the fluorescence intensity. (D) The effect of different pH on fluorescence quenching intensity.

(A) simultaneously detecting the fluorescence emission spectrum of aflD (0.1–500 nM) and AFB1 (0.1–500 ng/mL). (B) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 465 nm and aflD concentration. (C) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity at 595 nm and AFB1 concentration.
(A) The effect of mismatched aflD on aflD detection. (B) The effect of mycotoxins on AFB1 detection.
Schematic diagram of magnetically controlled fluorescence biosensor for simultaneous detection of AFB1 and aflD.
Comparison of the fabricated biosensor with reported biosensor for AFB1 or aflD genes.
showed the analysis results, and different concentrations of aflD and AFB1 in real corn and rice samples were detected by standard addition (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Magnetically controlled fluorescence biosensor for simultaneous detection of aflatoxin B1 and its toxin-producing gene aflD

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Grain Science and Technology
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) was considered as the most toxic natural compound and exists widely in the world, it is great significance to develop a high-performance sensor for simultaneously detection AFB1 and its toxin-producing gene aflD. In this paper, a magnetically controlled fluorescent sensor was developed, ssDNA1/ssDNA2 was modified on graphene quantum dots (GQDs)/Mn:ZnS quantum dots (QDs) as an energy donor, and cDNA/Apt modified by black hole quencher (BHQ) was coupled with MNPs as an energy acceptor, which leaded to fluorescence quenching through donor-acceptor energy transfer. Parts of fluorescent probes were separated from magnetic carriers when the sensor was incubated with the targets. After magnetic separation, the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe released from the supernatant was positively correlated with the target concentration. The simultaneous detection of aflD and AFB1 was realized. The sensor displayed a wide detection range (0.1–500 nM and 0.1–500 ng/mL) for aflD and AFB1, with detection limits of 0.021 nM and 0.038 ng/mL for aflD and AFB1. The magnetic fluorescence biosensor showed a good recovery rate in real rice flour and corn flour samples. The magnetic control fluorescence biosensor had the high sensitivity and specificity. This strategy provided a simple, efficient and sensitive method for food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxin is a toxic substance and widely found in people's food and animal feeds (Li et al., 2024a; Wang, Gu, Rong, et al., 2024). There are more than 20 kinds of aflatoxins discovered by scientists, and aflatoxins B1 (AFB1), aflatoxins B2, aflatoxins G1 and aflatoxins G2 was considered as the particularly dangerous molecule substance to humans and animals (Li et al., 2023; Wang, Luo, et al., 2022). Especially, AFB1 is regarded as the most common toxic compound due to its high adhesion to DNA and protein (Junsai et al., 2021; Wang, Zhao, et al., 2022). To date, precision instrument-based assays remain the dominant detection route for AFB1 including liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS),gas chromatography (GC),high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Feng et al., 2022; Xie et al., 2018). While these techniques offered excellent qualitative and quantitative measurements, they are time-consuming and costly, with the need for bulky instruments, complex and cumbersome sample preparation procedures and inadequately trained personnel (Zhu, Chu, et al., 2020). Therefore, it is meaningful to establish a simple, rapid and sensitive approaches for detecting AFB1 based on biosensor with the advantages of ease of operation, cost effectiveness, and reliability of results. If the possibility of aflatoxin production in samples can be predicted before aflatoxins are generated, it would reduce the contamination of aflatoxins in grain effectively.

The production of aflatoxin occurs in the secondary metabolism of aflatoxin-producing fungi, and involves the expression of aflD transcription gene, which is one of the constitutive genes of aflatoxin gene group in Aspergillus and other species Aspergillus molds (Alejandra et al., 2010; Iheanacho et al., 2014). The existence of aflD is related to the production of aflatoxin, indicating that the gene has the potential as a marker (Li et al., 2021; Samaneh et al., 2017). Therefore, the monitoring of aflD and AFB1 at the same time will be an efficient instrument to avoid the production of AFB1 amount and the enlargement of AFB1 widely. Aptamers are shorter nucleic acid (DNA or RNA) sequences with higher affinity for proteins, cells and small molecules (Rahimi et al., 2021; Wang, Qian, et al., 2019). Adaptive biosensor showed excellent properties in various fields of drug delivery system, pharmacy development, environmental hazards monitoring and foodborne contaminant (Park et al., 2023). Fluorescent aptamer or DNA biosensor displayed the outstanding advantages of simple operation, low cost, high luminosity, strong responsiveness and good stability, which was aroused great interest of researchers (Kweon et al., 2022). At present, researchers have made great contributions to the simultaneous detection of AFB1 or multiple mycotoxins. For example, Min Wei et al. designed cDNA with hairpin structure labeled with carboxyfluorescein and constructed a facile and innovative FRET strategy for the detection of aflatoxin B1 based on gold nanoparticles fluorescence quenching of material (Wei et al., 2020). Jiang et al. developed a magnetically controlled aptamer sensor for simultaneous OTA and FB1 by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (Jiang et al., 2020). However, there are few articles on detecting aflD and AFB1 at the same time. Our group designed a DNA-assisted fluorescent probe switch based on resonance energy transfer to detect aflD (Yu et al., 2024). It was an opportunity to develop a method to detect aflD and AFB1 simultaneously to improve detection efficiency, which was also directly involved in the biosynthesis pathway of AFB1 in certain fungi, we can monitor the early contaminant formation in grains and gain insights into the potential for toxin production even before significant amounts of the toxin are actually present.

The sensing strategy of biosensor based on functional high-performance materials possess the benefits of great sensitivity and specialization, which opened up a new way for mycotoxin detection (Hayat et al., 2022; Sharma et al., 2021). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are ideally suited as fluorescent probes because of their unique properties such as wide excitation spectra, narrow emission spectra, and photostability (Li et al., 2024c; Wang, Gu, Zhao, et al., 2024). ZnS QDs also showed the remarkable properties of conventional quantum dots and was used for biosensor fabrication. ZnS QDs was doped to generate impurity centers of electrons and holes interaction and improved the stability of fluorescence (Li et al., 2022; Mostafa et al., 2021). Mn:ZnS QDs inherited the basic advantages of ZnS QDs while having additional unique features due to the doping of Mn ions. They not only maintained the good crystallinity and homogeneous dispersion of ZnS QDs but also possessed the advantages of low toxicity and resistance to photobleaching, made them ideal for constructing the energy donor-acceptor pairs in our magnetically controlled fluorescent biosensor (Yuan et al., 2021). Black hole quencher (BHQ) is famous for its excellent quenching ability. Compared with other quenchers, BHQ reveals the wide absorption band, considerable extinction coefficient and the ability to re-emit in infrared region (Feng et al., 2022) Different types of BHQ (BHQ1, BHQ2 and BHQ3) can be selected to quench various of fluorescent probes due to their individual characteristics (Hu et al., 2021). The maximum absorption range of BHQ1 is around 480–580 nm and the range of BHQ3 is 620–730 nm, which demonstrated that BHQ1 and BHQ3 can sensitively quench the quantum dots in corresponding fluorescent emission through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (Zheng et al., 2020). Meanwhile, magnetic nano-material (MNPs) with favorable magnetic separation and enriched properties were used widely in biosensors to enhance the sensitivity and selectivity (Qian et al., 2018; Yao et al., 2021). By means of functionalization, the performance characteristics of quantum dots, BHQ and MNPs were fused to establish suitable biosensors, which were conducive to enhancing their detection sensitivity and efficiency.

In this paper, a magnetically controlled fluorescent aptamer sensor was developed based on double quantum dot signaling for simultaneously monitoring aflD and AFB1 for the first time. By modifying cDNA1/cDNA2 on GQDs/Mn:ZnS QDs as the energy donor, and coupling ssDNA/Apt modified by BHQ with MNPs as the energy acceptor, a magnetically controlled fluorescent biosensor was successfully constructed with the FRET between stable QDs and strong quenching BHQ. Since the highest affinity between aptamer and targets, some fluorescent probes were separated from the magnetic carrier when the sensor was incubated with targets. After magnetic separation, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of the fluorescent probe released from the supernatant was positively correlated with the target concentration of aflD and AFB1. This fabricated fluorescent biosensor displayed the high sensitivity, good specificity and fast separation by stable fluorescence performances of QDs and magnetic separation technology. Finally, this strategy was employed for real samples of rice and corn detection, which demonstrated the potential for treating other food contaminants and DNA/miRNAs-related diseases.

All chemicals are analytical reagent level and can be used with no further processing at the time of purchase. Tri (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (NH2C(CH2OH)3), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, 99%), manganese acetate (Mn(OAc)2), sodium hydroxide (MeOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), phosphoric acid (H2HPO4), sodium sulfide nonahydrate (Na2S•9H2O), zinc sulfate heptahydrate (ZnSO4•7H2O), trisodium citrate (Na3Cit), ferric chloride (FeCl3), sodium acetate trihydrate (NaAc•3H2O), citric acid (CA). 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), AFB1, OTA, FB1, ZEN, DON and ultrapure water is prepared from water passing through Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore ltd, Bedford, USA) every day. All DNA oligonucleotides used in this work were provided and purified by Sangon (Shanghai, China). The DNA sequence was heated to 95 °C for 10 min and then cooled to room temperature for at least 6 h for later use. Detailed sequence information of all DNA was shown in Table 1 .

All the fluorescence spectra of the samples were obtained by fluorescence spectrophotometer FS5. The size and morphology of Mn:ZnS QDs, GQDs and MNPs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) (FEI Talos F200X). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) measurement was carried out with Tensor-27 FTIR spectrometer (Bruker Company, Germany). The analysis of elements and functional groups was carried out on the ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS-Ultra DLD). Using zeta potentiometer Malvern Instruments Ltd (zeta sizer ver. 7.11, MAL1032660, England) to obtain zeta potential.

Preparation of Mn:ZnS QDs (QDs2) by Reflux Method according to the literature with minor modification (Labiadh et al., 2013). Briefly, in a round-bottomed flask, 2.5 mL of 0.2 M ZnSO4•7H2O, 200 μL of 0.1 M Mn (OAc)2, 174 μL MPA and 5 mL of 0.1 M Na2S•9H2O were combined. Distilled water was added to 40 mL. The pH value of solution was adjusted to 11 with 2 M NaOH. Next, the reaction was degassed with N2 gas for 40 min. After reflux was completed at 100 °C for 12 h, the solution was cooled and set aside. 10 mL of QDs2 stock solution was taken, and 20 ml of Li-proh was added to centrifuge for three times, and dried at 60 °C for later use.

By reducing FeCl3, On the basis of improved solvothermal reaction, MNPs was prepared (Shao et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021). In short, 0.25 g of Na3Cit and 0.8 g of FeCl3 were diffused in 25 mL of ethylene glycol, and 1.5 g of NaAc•3H2O was added to the above mixture under constant stirring at 600 rpm. The above solution was transferred to a reaction kettle, heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After that, the black product was dispersed in ethanol and water, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, repeated three times, and dried for later use.

Use a simple “bottom-up' method to synthesize GQDs (He et al., 2018). 2 g citric acid was put into a beaker and heated to 200 °C with a heating jacket. During the heating process, citric acid was liquefied first. After 30 min, the liquid's color shifted from colorless to pale yellow and finally to orange. This color change indicated that quantum dots was successfully prepared. The above solution was dialyzed with deionized water in a dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 1000 Da for 48 h. The excess citric acid was eliminated and the final solid GQDs (QDs1) were obtained by freeze-drying.

ssDNA modified by amino group was covalently bound to QDs1 and QDs2 modified by -COOH group, and QDs-ssDNA was produced with the assistance of EDC and NHS activation. In short, 3 mL of 20 mg/mL QDs1 and 5 mg/mL QDs2 were mixed with EDC (300 μL, 20 mM) and NHS (300 μL, 10 mM) and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h 300 μL of 10 μM ssDNA1/ssDNA2 was added to the above solutions, then incubated at a constant temperature of 37 °C for 12 h, centrifuged three times at 8000 rpm for 5 min to remove excess ssDNA, dispersed in 2 mL PBS buffer solution and stored at 4 °C before use (All reactions were carried out in 10 mM PBS buffer with pH = 7.4).

First, 80 μL of 10 mg/mL MNPs was mixed with EDC (10 μL, 20 mM) and NHS (10 μL, 10 mM), and incubated at 37 °C for 1 h. Then 10 μL of 100 μM aflD-cDNA-BHQ1 and AFB1-Apt-BHQ3 were added and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The unbound cDNA/Apt was removed by centrifugation (rotation speed: 8000 rpm, time: 5 min). MNPs-BHQ was washed three times with PBS buffer. Finally, MNPs-BHQ was resuspended in 900 μL PBS buffer and stored at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biosensor fabrication and the detection process
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 10 μL of MNPs-BHQ, 100 μL of QDs-ssDNA1 and 100 μL of QDs-ssDNA1 were added into a series of parallel centrifuge tubes, and then ssDNA1 hybridized with cDNA, ssDNA2 and Apt at 37 °C for 1 h to form the MNPs-BHQ-QDs complex. The mixture was magnetically separated under an external magnetic field for 1 min, washed with PBS buffer for three times, and finally added with 200 μL PBS for constant volume. Subsequently, 5 μL of different concentrations of aflD and AFB1 were added to 200 μL of MNPs-BHQ-QDs complex and incubated at 37 °C for 1h. Finally, the released marker QDs-ssDNA was collected by three magnetic separations and transferred to a 0.5 mL centrifuge tube. The change in fluorescence intensity in the wavelength range of 400–750 nm was measured by fluorescence spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of simultaneous detection of AFB1 and aflD
ce:section.ce:para: Modified amino ssDNA1 and ssDNA2 and functionalized QDs1 and QDs2 were used for simultaneous determination of aflD and AFB1 by fluorescence intensity changes. In Scheme 1 , the detection mechanism based on QDs1 and QDs2 was described in details. As shown in Scheme 1A, Peptide bonds were generated by the reaction between carboxyl groups on the surface of QDs and amino groups on the surface of ssDNA. Thus, QDs1-ssDNA1 and QDs2-ssDNA2 probes were successfully prepared. The dual fluorescent probe showed both emission peaks with maximum intensities of 465 nm and 595 nm, respectively. Firstly, quencher probes BHQ1 and BHQ3 were modified with cDNA and Apt at the 5′ end, respectively. The carboxyl-modified MNPs was conjugated with amino-modified cDNA1-BHQ1 and Apt-BHQ3 at the 3′ end, and the cDNA was complementary to a part of ssDNA (cDNA1 for ssDNA1 and Apt for ssDNA2). QDs1/2-ssDNA1-MNPs-BHQ were prepared and cleaned by magnetic separation. After aflD and AFB1 were added to MNs-BHQ-QDs, due to the specific recognition, cDNA/Apt was binded to the target through base complementary pairing to form a more stable double-stranded structure. The carboxyl-modified MNPs was conjugated with cDNA1-BHQ1 and Apt-BHQ3 modified with amino groups at the 3′ end, and the cDNA was complementary to a part of ssDNA (cDNA1 for ssDNA1 and Apt for ssDNA2). QDs1/2-ssDNA1-MNPs-BHQ was prepared and purified by magnetic separation. After adding aflD and AFB1 into MNs-BHQ-QDs, due to the specific recognition, cDNA/Apt was easier to bind to the target through base complementary pairing and formed a more stable duplex. The fluorescent probes were released into the supernatant as a result of that MNPs-cDNA/Apt bounded with targets, therefore, the quenched fluorescence was restored with the targets occurrence (Scheme 1B).

Fig. 1 A showed that excitation and emission wavelengths of QDs1 were 360 nm and 465 nm, respectively. Fig. 1B showed that QDs1 has good water solubility with uniform particle size distribution and small particle size. Illustration of Fig. 1B showed that its particle size was 2.167 nm. Fig. 1C showed that QDs1 were rich in carboxyl groups and can react well with amino groups of ssDNA (He et al., 2018). In order to further determine the elemental characteristics and composition of QDs1, the electronic energy spectrum was used for research and explanation. Fig. 1D mainly showed C1s (284.8 eV) and O1s (530 eV), and the quantum dot contains characteristic peaks of Na1s (1040 eV) and Cl2p (200 eV) due to dialysis. In Fig. 1E, C1s was decomposed into three peaks, and C1s mainly has characteristic peaks at 284.8, 286 and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-O-C and C=O respectively. As shown in Fig. 1F, O1s mainly has a characteristic peak of 532.5 eV, corresponding to O-H (Buatong et al., 2023).

Fig. 2 A showed the excitation and emission spectrum of QDs2, from which the excitation wavelength of QDs2 was 332 nm and the emission wavelength was 595 nm. The morphology of quantum dots was confirmed by transmission electron microscope. The TEM image in Fig. 2B showed that the synthesized QDs2 displayed almost spherical particles and was uniformly dispersed. Illustration of Fig. 2B showed that its particle size was 3.255 nm. Fig. 2C showed the FTIR spectrum of Mn:ZnS QDs (Hu et al., 2018). The O-H characteristic showed a broad peak in the range of 3500-3300 cm−1. It was possible that 1617 cm−1 and 1403 cm−1 were caused by the microstructure of the samples. The bands around 1567 and 1277 cm−1 were related to C=O and C–O, indicating that QDs was modified with -COOH. The band at 1105 cm−1 indicated a partially affected structure of ZnS by the doped Mn2+ impurity. The peak at 622 cm−1 was ascribed to the Zn-S stretching mode. The band at 490 cm−1 was attributed to Mn-O tensile vibration. The reason may be that some Mn2+ combined with O2− in hot solution to form MnO. In a word, the FTIR results confirmed that Mn2+ was successful doped into host lattice of ZnS.

QDs2 was characterized by XPS (Labiadh et al., 2013), as shown in Fig. 2D. The full spectrum showed that QDs have characteristic peaks at 161.5, 284.8, 530, 638.7 and 1021.7 eV, and the surface of the quantum dot mainly contains five elements: C, O, S, Mn and Zn. As shown in Fig. 2E, S2p mainly has a characteristic peak of 161 eV. As can be seen from Fig. 2F, C1s mainly has characteristic peaks at 284.8, 286 and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C-C, C-O-C and C=O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2G, O1s mainly has a characteristic peak of 532.5 eV, corresponding to O-H. As shown in Fig. 2H, there were two characteristic peaks at 641.4 and 654.2 eV, corresponding to Mn2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2 respectively, As shown in Fig. 2I, Zn2p has two characteristic peaks at 1044.98 eV and 1022.36 eV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MNPs and coupling characterization
ce:section.ce:para: Through TEM observation, Fig. 3 A showed that MNPs has good uniformity and dispersion, and its morphology was spherical. Illustration of Fig. 3A showed that its particle size was 244 nm. The synthesized MNPs was characterized by FT-IR (Shao et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 3B, the infrared spectrum of carboxyl modified MNPs has two characteristic bands of Fe-O expansion and carboxylic acid groups. The absorption peaks at 580 and 430 cm−1 were ascribed to Fe-O stretching. At about 1652 and 1396 cm−1, palpable bands attributed to carboxylic acid groups can be seen. The absorption peaks at 1068 and 3400 cm−1 can be attributed to C-H vibration and O-H vibration, respectively. The elemental analysis of MNPs was carried out by XPS, as shown in Fig. 3C. The full spectrum of MNPs showed that there were characteristic peaks at 530, 284.8, 200 and 710 eV. On the surface, the quantum dot mainly contains four elements: O, C, Cl and Fe. As shown in Fig. 3D, C1s mainly has characteristic peaks at 284.4, 286 and 288.5 eV, corresponding to C-C and C=O, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3E, O1s mainly has a characteristic peak of 532.5 eV, corresponding to O-H. As shown in Fig. 3F, there were two diffraction peaks at 710.48 and 723.78 eV, corresponding to Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 respectively. Trough measuring and analyzing the zeta potentials of QDs1, QDs, MNPs, QDs1-ssDNA1, QDs-ssDNA2 and MNPs-BHQ, it demonstrated that the nano-materials coupled with DNA successfully and achieved the more negative zeta potentials than the nano-materials.

In order to obtain effective operating conditions for simultaneously detecting aflD and AFB1, the influence parameters such as the concentration ratio of quantum dots, the concentration of ssDNA1/2, and the type of buffer solution and the pH of the PBS buffer were optimized. All error lines were obtained by three individual surveys. Firstly, as shown in Fig. 4 A, the concentration ratios of QDs1:QDs2 were set at 5:2, 2:1, 5:3, 4:1, and 10:1, and it was proved that the optimal concentration ratio was 4:1. 10 μL MNPs-BHQ was added to different concentrations of QDs-ssDNA to study the effect of QDs-ssDNA concentration on fluorescence quenching. It can be seen that when the concentration of cDNA1 and cDNA2 increased to 0.5 and 0.8 μM respectively, the fluorescence intensity was no longer quenched (Fig. 4B). Therefore, the optimum concentrations of ssDNA1 and ssDNA2 were 0.5 μM and 0.8 μM respectively. As shown in Fig. 4C, in order to explore the influence of buffer solution on fluorescence recovery, the best buffer solution was PBS, and the fluorescence recovery was the strongest in the presence of PBS. As shown in Fig. 4D, in order to explore the effect of pH of PBS buffer solution on fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence quenching effect was the best when pH was 7.4. Therefore, 0.5 μM ssDNA1 and 0.8 μM ssDNA2 were selected for all experiments, and PBS was selected as buffer with pH of 7.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simultaneous detection of aflD and AFB1
ce:section.ce:para: Then aflD and AFB1 were simultaneously detected to prove our ability of one-step double detection. Under the optimized conditions, aflD and AFB1 were incubated with MNPs-BHQ-QDs1/QDs2. The fluorescence intensity of the supernatant was measured at a single excitation wavelength. As shown in Fig. 5 A, in the presence of aflD and AFB1, the fluorescence intensities at 465 nm and 595 nm recovered significantly. A positive linear relationship can be established between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of aflD and AFB1 (Fig. 5B and C). R2 of aflD and AFB1 were 0.99136 and 0.9954, respectively. The detection limits of aflD and AFB1 were 0.021 nM and 0.038 ng/mL respectively. Obviously, this one-step method for simultaneously detecting aflD and AFB1 was sensitive and reliable. Meanwhile, the comparison with the reported biosensor for aflD or AFB1 was listed in Table 2 , it can be seen that this fabricated biosensor performed a wider linear range and the lower LOD. And it was used for detecting the two targets at the same time, which was conducive to improve detection efficiency and enabled early warning of contaminants.

Specificity testing is a key step in evaluating the proposed approach. To validate the specificity of aflD probe switch, the mismatched DNA sequence was added to the prepared MNPs-BHQ-QDs solution and the fluorescence intensity was measured. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the sensor showed maximum fluorescence recovery when fully complementary DNA sequences were used, which was attributed to the increased specific recognition between QDs-ssDNA and MNPs-BHQ by DNA base pairing. As the number of mismatched bases increased, the extent of fluorescence recovery was decreased. Additionally, random DNA sequences resulted in insignificant fluorescence recovery, and the fluorescence intensity was close to that of the blank group. The results showed that this method has good specificity. In our research, to analyze the developed biosensor for AFB1 selectivity and sensitivity, common toxins (DON, OTA, T-2, AFB2, FB1) were specifically analyzed. As shown in Fig. 6B, DON, OTA, T-2, ZEN and FB1 basically do not have fluorescence recovery in this system.

First of all, washed the corn and rice flour with distilled water to remove any surface debris, dust, or loosely attached contaminants. After washing, allowed the rice to air dry completely. Then, 1 μM aflD were added to 0.1 g solid corn and rice flour samples respectively, used the nucleic acid extraction kit box to collection the aflD and spiked the final solution at 1 nM, 50 nM and 500 nM. Meanwhile, 1 μg/mL AFB1 were also added to 0.1 g solid corn and rice flour samples respectively, dried at room temperature, added to 1 mL extractant (methanol:water = 7:3 v/v), shaken for 10 min, centrifuged at 12000 r/min for 5 min, and the supernatant was diluted with PBS. The final spiked solution concentrations of AFB1 in corn and rice were 1 ng/mL, 50 ng/mL and 500 ng/mL. Then, added 100 μl of prepared AFB1 and 100 μl of aflD to 210 μL MNPs-BHQ-QDs solution for more analysis, recorded their fluorescent intensity. As shown in Table 3 , which demonstrated good recoveries, indicated that this experiment was highly practical. The strategy was verified by Real-time PCR and ELISA, it showed the same results and performed the reliability of the biosensor for aflD and AFB1 simultaneous detection in real samples.

Based on ssDNA-labeled double quantum dots and BHQ-modified MNPs, we developed a magnetically controlled fluorescence biosensor, which can be used to detect aflD and AFB1 simultaneously. In this sensor, the fluorescence was restored by adding aflD and AFB1, and the existence of aflD and AFB1 was successfully confirmed. LODs of aflD and AFB1 are as low as 0.021 nM and 0.038 ng/mL, respectively. This strategy had the advantages of low background signal, high sensitivity, good specificity and simple operation. In addition, through the spiked detection of aflD and AFB1 in corn and rice, it was proved that this method showed excellent practicability in corn and rice food. To some extent, this method was helpful for early detection and early intervention of aflatoxin or contaminated food.

Yaqi Li: Writing – review & editing. Shuangfeng Peng: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Xin Chen: Formal analysis, Data curation. Danni Sun: Formal analysis, Data curation. Xiang Zuo: Investigation. Chang Liu: Validation. Qi Zhang: Validation. Shijie Li: Methodology. Hua Ye: Methodology. Dezhao Kong: Supervision.
This research complied with the Ethical approval and did not involve animal experimentation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Public Welfare Application Research Project of Huzhou (No. 2024GZ33), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31901799, 32372417, 22406069), Chinese Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2021M692370), National Key Research and Development Program of China(No.2022YFD2101203).
